policy_statement: Partner with Colorado Springs Utilities and school districts to support education and incentives for water and energy conservation and environmental quality best practices.
mechanism_description: The strategy partners with the utility and school districts to support educational efforts and provide incentives that encourage water and energy conservation, reducing overall resource use and associated emissions.
primary_causal_pathway: Same service (comfort, lighting, water services) → ↓ energy/water input → ↓ fuel combustion & grid generation → ↓ GHG emissions
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Households, students, and institutions adopt conservation behaviors and/or install efficiency measures due to education campaigns and incentive programs (e.g., using less hot water, reducing wasteful water use, improving operational practices).  
2) Mediating physical process: Lower water withdrawals, treatment, pumping, distribution, and wastewater processing reduce electricity use; lower end-use energy (and hot-water demand) reduces on-site fuel combustion and grid electricity generation.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced CO₂ (and other GHG) emissions from power plants and building fuel use; potentially additional reductions from avoided water system energy and related upstream emissions.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced energy demand (directly and via reduced water system energy use), leading to lower GHG emissions from energy production.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Education + incentives → reduced energy/water consumption → reduced energy production and fuel combustion → reduced GHG emissions.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While emissions reduction is the ultimate climate benefit, the defining mechanism is delivering the same services with fewer resource inputs (energy and water), which fits Resource Efficiency more directly than a pure Mitigation label.  
3) Edge cases applied: Some programs may also improve drought resilience (Adaptation) by lowering demand during shortages, but that is not the dominant climate effect across this mechanism; likewise, “environmental quality best practices” could include non-energy actions, yet the stated causal chain centers on conservation-driven demand reduction.  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy framed as conservation education and incentives intended to reduce energy and/or water use primarily operates through demand reduction and efficiency (resource input reduction), so Resource Efficiency is consistently primary.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy is chiefly “energy efficiency,” “water efficiency,” “behavior change,” or “utility DSM” is policy-specific and should be assigned based on implementation details and evidence at the individual policy level.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the incentives primarily fund on-site renewables or fuel-switching (not just reduced use), the dominant pathway could shift toward direct Mitigation; if framed mainly for drought emergency preparedness, Adaptation could become primary. In the stated mechanism, neither appears dominant.
key_indicators: “Partner with Colorado Springs Utilities and school districts”; “support educational efforts”; “provide incentives”; “water and energy conservation”; “environmental quality best practices”
co_benefits: Improved environmental quality (e.g., reduced local pollution), reduced water demand and stress on water systems, cost savings for households/schools, conservation awareness/education outcomes.
instance_edge_case_notes: Includes both education (information-disclosure/outreach) and incentives; classified as incentive-program because “provide incentives” is an explicit instrument alongside education. No binding requirements or quantified targets are stated.
